incident_details.railroad_mile_post_or_station_facility: Downtown Albuquerque Station – Northbound Platform, near Track 1
incident_details.railroad_crossing_street: 1st Street SW & Central Ave SW, Albuquerque, NM 87102
incident_details.weather_conditions: Clear skies, dry conditions, temperature approximately 82°F, light breeze from the west
incident_details.consist_locomotive_car_number: One MP36PH-3C locomotive (unit #103) and three bi-level passenger coaches (cars #201, #204, and #209) in push-pull configuration
incident_details.condition_and_number_of_equipment_if_inv: Station platform tactile edge strip loose and partially detached along approximately 6–8 feet of the northbound platform edge, directly in front of mid-coach door of car #204. No visible cones, barricades, or warning tape present at the time of the incident. Platform lighting and public address system otherwise functioning normally.
incident_details.conductors: Primary conductor: Michael J. Ortega; assistant conductor: Lisa M. Carrillo (names obtained from business cards and subsequent email follow-up).
incident_details.engineer: Name unknown at time of filing; referred to by staff only as the "northbound engineer on duty" for Train 708 on 2025-09-18.

claimant_information.claimants_name_adress:
Elena M. Rodriguez
4127 Sandia Crest Drive NE
Albuquerque, NM 87110

claim_description.incident_description_fault:
On Thursday, September 18, 2025, at approximately 5:42 p.m., I, Elena Maria Rodriguez, was boarding New Mexico Rail Runner Train 708 northbound at the Downtown Albuquerque Station on my way home to the Los Ranchos/Journal Center area after work. I am a regular commuter and have been using the Rail Runner three to four days per week for the past several years.

I arrived on the northbound platform around 5:35 p.m. and stood roughly in the middle portion of the platform, where the second passenger car typically stops. When Train 708 arrived, the doors of car #204 (the middle coach) opened directly in front of where I was standing. As the conductor announced boarding, I stepped forward with a small laptop backpack on my shoulders and a reusable grocery tote in my left hand. There was a moderate crowd of other commuters boarding and disembarking at the same time.

As I moved toward the open door, my right foot landed on a portion of the yellow tactile warning strip at the platform edge that was loose and partially detached from the concrete. The edge of the raised rubberized strip was curled upward several inches, creating an unexpected lip. My foot caught on this raised edge, and because I was mid-step and the crowd behind me was moving forward, I could not recover my balance. I pitched forward sharply, twisting my right ankle and falling sideways onto my left knee and hip, striking the metal threshold at the train door and then the interior floor just inside the doorway.

Several passengers behind me stumbled as they tried to avoid stepping on me, and one gentleman helped pull me fully inside the car so the doors would not close on me. The conductor, who identified himself as Michael Ortega, immediately came over to ask if I was okay. I told him that my ankle and knee hurt badly and that I felt a sharp pain in my lower back and left wrist where I had tried to catch myself. He asked if I needed an ambulance. At that moment I was embarrassed and shaken and said I thought I could sit down and see how I felt once the train was moving.

After I was seated, Mr. Ortega briefly stepped back onto the platform. From my seat by the window, I watched him look down at the area where I had tripped. I could clearly see the yellow tactile strip buckled up and separated from the platform surface for several feet. There were no orange cones, no barricades, and no warning signs posted along that section of the platform. At no point prior to my fall did any Rail Runner employee make an announcement warning passengers about a hazardous condition on the platform.

Within a few minutes of departure, my right ankle began to swell noticeably and became very stiff, and my left knee throbbed with pain. I also developed a dull ache and tightness in my lower back that grew worse during the 15–20 minute ride. When I attempted to stand to exit at Los Ranchos/Journal Center Station, it was extremely painful to put weight on my right foot. Another passenger had to help me down the steps and onto the platform.

My husband picked me up from the station and took me directly to Presbyterian Kaseman Urgent Care on Wyoming Blvd. There, X-rays were taken of my right ankle and left knee. The doctor diagnosed a moderate right ankle sprain, a contusion and possible meniscal irritation in my left knee, and lumbar strain. I was prescribed anti-inflammatory medication and muscle relaxers, instructed to use crutches for at least a week, given a brace for my ankle, and referred to physical therapy if my condition did not improve in 10–14 days.

I believe the New Mexico Rail Runner Express and the responsible state agency are at fault because the hazardous condition that caused my fall appears to have been a preexisting maintenance issue that should have been identified and repaired or at least clearly marked. The tactile warning strip is a permanent safety feature meant to protect passengers from falling onto the tracks. Allowing it to become partially detached and curled upward directly in the passenger boarding area, without any cones, warning tape, or signage, created an unreasonable and foreseeable tripping hazard.

Given that this is a major station with a high volume of daily passengers, the Rail Runner has a duty to regularly inspect the condition of its platforms, especially the boarding areas, and to promptly correct or clearly mark any dangerous conditions. Based on the visible discoloration and dirt under the raised portion of the strip, it appears that the separation had been occurring for some time, not just that day. A reasonably careful inspection and maintenance program should have caught this problem before someone was hurt. Even if immediate repair was not possible, the agency should have placed cones or temporary barriers around the defective section and directed passengers to board at a safer location.

Additionally, no announcements were made about any construction, maintenance, or hazards on the northbound platform. The area was not roped off, and passengers were actively directed to board at that door by the normal train stopping position and conductor’s hand signals. In my view, the Rail Runner effectively invited us to board through an area that they either knew or should have known was unsafe.

As a direct result of this fall, I have incurred medical expenses, missed work, and ongoing pain and limitation in my daily activities. I am submitting this claim because I believe the state agency responsible for the Rail Runner failed to maintain its station facilities in a reasonably safe condition and failed to warn passengers of a known or reasonably discoverable hazard, which directly caused my injuries and related losses.
claim_description.injury_or_damage_description:
Bodily Injury:
Immediately after the fall, I experienced sharp pain in my right ankle and left knee, as well as soreness in my lower back and left wrist. By the time I arrived at urgent care (approximately 45 minutes after the incident), my right ankle was visibly swollen and tender to the touch, and I had difficulty bearing weight on it. The urgent care physician diagnosed a Grade II sprain of the right ankle with ligament strain, a contusion and suspected soft-tissue injury to the left knee (possible meniscal irritation, though no fracture), and lumbar muscle strain from the twisting motion and impact.

I was provided with an ankle brace and instructed to use crutches for 7–10 days, to keep weight off the ankle as much as possible, and to elevate and ice the area several times per day. I was prescribed naproxen for inflammation and a short course of muscle relaxers for my back. The doctor recommended follow-up with my primary care provider and, if symptoms persisted, a referral for physical therapy.

Over the following two weeks, I continued to experience significant pain and stiffness, especially when walking, climbing stairs, or standing for more than about 15–20 minutes. My left knee developed a deep aching sensation, particularly at night, and I had difficulty sleeping comfortably. I also experienced ongoing lower back tightness that made bending, lifting, and even sitting at my desk for long periods very uncomfortable. I have since had two follow-up appointments with my primary care physician at Presbyterian Medical Group. She confirmed ongoing soft-tissue injury and referred me to physical therapy, which I have started attending twice a week.

These injuries have meaningfully interfered with my daily life. I work as a project coordinator for a small architecture firm in downtown Albuquerque, and my job normally requires commuting by train, walking several blocks between the station and the office, and occasionally visiting job sites. For the first week after the incident, I was unable to go into the office at all and had to work from home on a reduced schedule, missing two full days of work and several partial days due to medical appointments and pain. Even now, more than three weeks after the fall, I still limp slightly, avoid stairs whenever possible, and cannot comfortably carry more than a light bag without aggravating my back.

At home, I have had difficulty performing routine tasks such as cooking, cleaning, and doing laundry. My husband and teenage daughter have had to take on most of the household chores. I normally enjoy walking our dog in the evenings and attending a weekend yoga class; I have been unable to do either since the incident. I also had to cancel a planned weekend trip to visit my parents in Las Cruces because sitting in the car for extended periods was too painful for my back and knee.

Property Damage:
During the fall, I landed partially on my left side and wrist while still gripping my reusable grocery tote, which contained my personal items and work laptop. My 14-inch work-issued Dell laptop (approximate replacement value $1,200) was in a padded sleeve inside the bag. After I got home that evening, I discovered a visible crack along the bottom left corner of the laptop casing and a hairline crack in the screen extending about 3 inches diagonally from the lower edge. The laptop now intermittently flickers and sometimes fails to boot properly. Our office IT staff have advised that, based on the damage, it may need to be replaced entirely rather than repaired.

Additionally, the screen of my personal smartphone (an iPhone 13) was chipped on the upper right corner, and there are new scratches along the side of the case that were not present before the incident. While the phone remains functional, the damage is cosmetic but permanent. The reusable tote bag I was carrying also tore at the handle seam, making it unusable.

In summary, I have sustained documented bodily injuries (right ankle sprain, left knee contusion/soft-tissue injury, and lumbar strain), ongoing pain and mobility limitations, and damage to personal property (work laptop, personal smartphone, and tote bag). I am seeking compensation for my medical expenses (urgent care visit, imaging, follow-up appointments, prescribed medications, and physical therapy), lost wages from missed work, and the repair or replacement of my damaged belongings, as well as consideration for the pain, inconvenience, and disruption this incident has caused in my daily life.
